planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; small Solomon's seal spreads steadily by rhizomes to form colonies and is best planted in generous drifts of at least 5–7 rhizomes.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; small Solomon's seal spreads steadily by rhizomes to form colonies and provides four seasons of interest — emerging foliage in spring, hanging white flowers in late spring, blue-black berries in summer, and golden-yellow fall color. Polygonatum biflorum is the smaller native eastern North American species of Solomon's seal (compared to the great Solomon's seal, P. commutatum), producing arching stems 18–36 inches long with alternate, oval, parallel-veined leaves and paired, pendant, tubular, white to cream flowers dangling from the leaf axils in late spring, followed by glaucous blue-black berries beloved by wildlife. The common name comes from the circular, seal-like scars left on the rhizome where old stems have attached; the rhizome was used medicinally and the young shoots are edible. Outstanding for naturalizing in shaded woodland gardens.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; great Solomon's seal is a bold, architectural species reaching 3–5 feet in height and requires ample space.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; great Solomon's seal requires generous spacing as it reaches 3–5 feet in height and spreads steadily to form large, impressive colonies over time. Polygonatum commutatum is the largest native eastern North American Solomon's seal, an impressive, bold-textured perennial with stout, strongly arching stems bearing large, oval, parallel-veined leaves and 2–8 pendant, greenish-white, tubular flowers per leaf node in late spring followed by dark blue-black berries in summer; the dramatic fall color is a rich, buttery yellow. It is one of the finest and most impressive native shade perennials for large-scale plantings under trees, along fence lines, and in naturalized woodland gardens; it combines well with ostrich ferns, hostas, and ligularia for a bold, tropical-looking shade planting.

planting.note: Sow direct at or just after last frost in moist, shaded soil; jewel weed self-seeds prolifically and will often naturalize in moist, shaded stream margins and woodland gardens with no intervention once established.

directions: Sow direct at last frost date in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; jewel weed self-seeds so abundantly that once established it will return year after year without intervention — it is best established by purchasing transplants or directing seeds into the garden in fall for spring germination. Impatiens capensis is a native North American annual wildflower of moist, shaded stream banks, woodland margins, and seeps; it produces succulent, blue-green stems bearing oval, toothed leaves and a profusion of small, orange-spotted, spurred flowers from mid-summer to frost that are intensely attractive to ruby-throated hummingbirds on their southward migration. The ripe seed pods explode forcefully when touched, dispersing seeds several feet (giving rise to "touch-me-not" and providing the genus name Impatiens). The juice of crushed stems is a traditional folk remedy for poison ivy rash and stinging nettle; while scientific evidence is limited, the plant often grows near both and is a useful companion to know.

planting.note: Sow direct at or just after last frost in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; pale touch-me-not often grows alongside orange jewelweed (I. capensis) in moist wooded areas.

directions: Sow direct at last frost in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; pale touch-me-not self-seeds prolifically in favorable conditions and once established naturalizes reliably without further intervention. Impatiens pallida is the yellow-flowered counterpart of the orange jewelweed (I. capensis) and is found in similar moist, shaded habitats across eastern North America; it produces succulent, translucent stems and pale-yellow, lightly spotted, spurred flowers from mid-summer to frost that are less intensely colored than the orange species but equally attractive to hummingbirds and long-tongued bumblebees. Like its relative, the seed pods explode when ripe, dispersing seeds vigorously. The two species frequently grow side by side and make a complementary pair in naturalized, moist woodland gardens; pale touch-me-not tends to prefer slightly less acidic, slightly less wet conditions than orange jewelweed. Stems can be used in the same folk remedies as I. capensis.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; false hellebore emerges very early in spring and is a bold architectural plant, but all parts are extremely toxic — never allow contact with eyes, mouth, or open wounds.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade near streams, bogs, and moist woodland; false hellebore requires consistently high moisture and is best in permanently wet or seepy sites. Veratrum viride is one of the most dramatic and bold-textured native wildflowers of eastern North American and Pacific Northwest mountain forests; it emerges very early in spring as spectacular, tightly pleated, corn-like leaves that unfurl to produce a towering, 3–6-foot-tall stem bearing large, plume-like panicles of small, yellowish-green, star-shaped flowers in mid-summer. The dramatic tropical appearance belies the plant's extreme cold-hardiness and cold-zone nativity; it is outstanding for bold foliage effect in moist, shaded woodland gardens. ALL PARTS OF THIS PLANT ARE EXTREMELY TOXIC — among the most poisonous native plants in North America, causing severe neurological symptoms if ingested. Handle with gloves and keep away from children and pets.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in a wide range of soils from dry to moist, full sun to full shade; common greenbrier is extremely adaptable but its thorny stems make placement important — best in naturalized areas or as wildlife cover.

directions: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in any reasonably fertile, well-drained to moist soil in full sun to full shade; common greenbrier climbs by tendrils and should be given a sturdy support or allowed to clamber through shrubs in naturalized garden areas, as the stout, prickly stems are difficult to remove once established. Smilax rotundifolia is a native vine of eastern North American forests, woodland edges, and disturbed areas; it produces stout, green, thorny stems that climb vigorously by paired tendrils, bearing glossy, round to heart-shaped, evergreen to semi-evergreen leaves, small, greenish, insignificant flowers in spring, and clusters of blue-black berries in fall that are important wildlife food for many species of birds and mammals. The young shoot tips, new leaves, and rhizome tips are edible and have been used as food by Native Americans; the berries are edible but insipid. Best used in naturalized, wildlife-friendly gardens where its aggressive colonizing habit is an asset rather than a problem.
